cards.q: Define pneumonia.
cards.a: Inflammation of the parenchyma of the lung caused by microorganisms.

cards.q: What does the lung parenchyma consist of?
cards.a: Respiratory bronchioles, alveolar ducts, alveolar sacs, alveoli and interstitial tissue.

cards.q: What is the difference between pneumonia and pneumonitis/alveolitis?
cards.a: Pneumonia is caused by microorganisms; pneumonitis or alveolitis is caused by chemical or physical injury such as burns.

cards.q: What does 'resolution may be partial or complete' imply clinically?
cards.a: Complete resolution restores normal lung; partial resolution leaves permanent damage and can lead to bronchiectasis.

cards.q: Classify pneumonia based on probable origin.
cards.a: Community acquired; hospital acquired (nosocomial/healthcare associated); aspiration; and pneumonia in the immunocompromised.

cards.q: Classify pneumonia based on anatomical involvement/pattern of distribution.
cards.a: Bronchopneumonia; and lobar or segmental pneumonia.

cards.q: Classify pneumonia based on type of infecting organism.
cards.a: Viral, bacterial, fungal, atypical and parasitic.

cards.q: Classify pneumonia based on the actual infecting organism.
cards.a: Pneumococcal, streptococcal, staphylococcal; Haemophilus influenzae; and tuberculous.

cards.q: How high does pneumonia rank as a cause of childhood illness?
cards.a: It ranks high amongst the causes of childhood morbidity and mortality.

cards.q: Which age group is most frequently affected by pneumonia?
cards.a: Infants and toddlers, more than older children.

cards.q: What is the seasonal pattern of pneumonia in Nigeria?
cards.a: It occurs throughout the year but is more common during the rainy and harmattan seasons.

cards.q: Why is pneumonia usually more severe in less privileged communities?
cards.a: Because the risk factors cluster in poverty — no exclusive breastfeeding, malnutrition, overcrowding, indoor smoke and delayed presentation.

cards.q: How often does a child die of pneumonia globally?
cards.a: Every 43 seconds.

cards.q: How many under-5 children did pneumonia kill in 2021?
cards.a: More than 700,000 — around 2,000 every day (UNICEF data).

cards.q: What percentage of all under-5 deaths does pneumonia account for?
What is the global incidence of childhood pneumonia?
cards.a: Over 1,400 cases per 100,000 children, or 1 case per 71 children every year.

cards.q: List the risk factors for pneumonia.
cards.a: Lack of breastfeeding especially exclusive; malnutrition including vitamin A deficiency; poor socio-economic status; large family size; young age especially under 2 years; low birth weight; overcrowding; previous illness (HIV, sickle cell anaemia, congenital lung/airway disorders); and indoor and outdoor pollution including smoking.

cards.q: Why does vitamin A deficiency predispose to pneumonia?
cards.a: Vitamin A maintains the respiratory epithelium and cilia; deficiency damages mucociliary clearance.

cards.q: How does breastfeeding protect the lung?
cards.a: Breast milk supplies maternal secretory IgA, one of the lung's own defences, at an age when the infant cannot make enough of its own.

cards.q: What is the most common cause of pneumonia in children overall?
cards.a: Viruses — accounting for approximately 50% at all ages but up to 80% in children under 2 years.

cards.q: What is the commonest single virus causing pneumonia?
cards.a: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV).

cards.q: List the bacterial causes of pneumonia in neonates.
cards.a: Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, Group B streptococcus and Staphylococcus aureus.

cards.q: Which organism accounts for over 70% of bacterial pneumonia in the 3 months to 3 years age group?
cards.a: Streptococcus pneumoniae.

cards.q: Besides Strep pneumoniae, what bacterium is important in the 3 months to 3 years group?
cards.a: Haemophilus influenzae.

cards.q: List the bacterial causes of pneumonia in children over 3 years.
cards.a: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Streptococcus pyogenes, Bordetella pertussis and Staphylococcus aureus.

cards.q: Why do neonates get E. coli, Klebsiella and Group B strep pneumonia?
cards.a: These are gut and genital tract organisms acquired during passage through the birth canal, not inhaled — so neonatal pneumonia is usually part of systemic sepsis.

cards.q: Name the atypical organisms causing pneumonia.
cards.a: Mycoplasma, chlamydia, Pneumocystis jiroveci and moraxella.

cards.q: Which atypical organism is implicated in neonates?
cards.a: Chlamydia trachomatis.

cards.q: Which atypical organism is implicated in the over-3-years group?
cards.a: Mycoplasma pneumoniae.

cards.q: Which atypical organism is implicated in HIV/immunocompromised children?
cards.a: Pneumocystis jiroveci.

cards.q: Why does mycoplasma pneumonia not respond to penicillin?
cards.a: Mycoplasma has no cell wall, so it needs a macrolide instead.

cards.q: List the fungal causes of pneumonia.
cards.a: Coccidioides, Aspergillus, Cryptococcus and Histoplasma.

cards.q: List the parasitic causes of pneumonia.
cards.a: Ascaris, toxoplasma and schistosoma.

cards.q: In what proportion of pneumonias is no pathogen identified?
cards.a: In 20-60% of cases.

cards.q: Why is treatment of pneumonia usually empiric?
cards.a: Because no pathogen is identified in 20-60% of cases and blood culture yields only 20-30%, so age is the main guide to the likely organism.

cards.q: Name the three physiologic defence mechanisms that keep the lower respiratory tract sterile.
cards.a: Mucociliary clearance; secretory immunoglobulin A (IgA); and clearing of the airway by coughing.

cards.q: Name the immunologic defence mechanisms of the lung.
cards.a: Macrophages present in alveoli and bronchioles; secretory IgA; and other immunoglobulins.

cards.q: By what four routes do pathogens enter the lower respiratory tract?
cards.a: Inhalation of airborne droplets; descending from URTI or another LRTI; aspiration; and haematogenous spread.

cards.q: Where do organisms first establish themselves in the lung?
cards.a: In the small bronchioles and alveoli, from where they spread to other parts of the lungs.

cards.q: Which immune cells engulf the pathogens and release cytokines in pneumonia?
cards.a: Alveolar macrophages, neutrophils, lymphocytes and eosinophils.

cards.q: What does the inflammatory response (apoptosis, vascular permeability with oedema, cytokines) cause?
cards.a: Cell destruction and alveolar congestion, which interrupts the exchange of oxygen across the alveolar membranes.

cards.q: On what three factors do the virulence and severity of pneumonia depend?
cards.a: The health of the individual, the causative microorganism, and the size of the inoculum.

cards.q: Name the four stages of inflammation in pneumonia.
cards.a: Vascular congestion; red hepatization; grey hepatization; and resolution.

cards.q: When does vascular congestion occur and what are its features?
cards.a: Within 24 hours; marked capillary engorgement/congestion and alveolar oedema, with protein-rich fluid containing numerous bacteria and few neutrophils. Produces clear sputum.

cards.q: What type of sputum is produced in vascular congestion?
cards.a: Clear sputum.

cards.q: When does red hepatization occur and what are its features?
cards.a: 2nd-4th day; many RBCs, neutrophils, desquamated epithelial cells and fibrin enter the alveoli; lung appears red, liver-like. Produces rusty sputum.

cards.q: What type of sputum is produced in red hepatization?
cards.a: Rusty sputum.

cards.q: Why is the sputum rusty in red hepatization?
cards.a: Because red blood cells enter the alveoli and are partly broken down.

cards.q: When does grey hepatization occur and what are its features?
cards.a: 4th-6th day; alveoli filled with fibrinopurulent exudate of haemolysed RBCs, degranulated neutrophils and macrophages; haemosiderin gives a grey-brown to yellow colour and firm consistency; lungs no longer congested.

cards.q: Why does the lung turn grey in grey hepatization?
cards.a: Haemoglobin from the haemolysed red cells is broken down to haemosiderin, a grey-brown pigment.

cards.q: When does resolution occur and what happens?
cards.a: 8th-9th day; resolution of inflammatory exudates and restoration of pulmonary architecture by the breakdown of exudates by enzymes.

cards.q: What does the word 'hepatization' mean?
cards.a: That the lung has become as solid and firm as liver (hepar = liver), because the alveoli are packed with exudate instead of air.

cards.q: Define lobar pneumonia.
cards.a: A type of pneumonia involving one or more lobes or part of a lobe of the lungs, in which the consolidation is virtually homogenous.

cards.q: List the bacterial causes of lobar pneumonia.
cards.a: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Staphylococcus pyogenes, Streptococcus pyogenes, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Pseudomonas, Proteus species and Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

cards.q: List the symptoms of lobar pneumonia.
cards.a: Fever, rigours, productive cough, breathlessness, anorexia, pleuritic chest pain, restlessness, cyanosis, upper abdominal pain, vomiting and diarrhoea, and occasionally haemoptysis.

cards.q: List the signs of lobar pneumonia.
cards.a: Ill, anxious, restless, toxic, cyanosed child; tachycardia, dyspnoea, tachypnoea; central trachea with diminished chest expansion over the affected lobe; dull percussion notes; increased tactile and vocal fremitus on the affected side; diminished then bronchial breath sounds, later replaced by crepitations.

cards.q: Why is percussion dull in lobar pneumonia?
cards.a: Because the consolidated tissue is solid; solid tissue does not resonate the way air-filled lung does.

cards.q: Why is tactile and vocal fremitus increased over consolidation?
cards.a: Because solid consolidated tissue conducts sound better than air-filled lung.

cards.q: Why does the trachea remain central in lobar pneumonia?
cards.a: Because consolidation replaces air with fluid inside the lung without changing its volume — nothing pushes or pulls the trachea.

cards.q: Why is there pleuritic chest pain in lobar pneumonia?
cards.a: Because a whole lobe reaches the parietal pleura, which has pain fibres; inflammation there causes sharp pain worse on inspiration and coughing.

cards.q: Why may lobar pneumonia cause abdominal pain, vomiting and diarrhoea?
cards.a: Lower lobe pneumonia irritates the diaphragmatic pleura, referring pain to the abdomen; and systemic toxaemia causes vomiting and loose stools.

cards.q: Define bronchopneumonia.
cards.a: Pneumonia with patchy consolidation throughout a lobe or the lungs.

cards.q: What is the commonest cause of bronchopneumonia?
cards.a: Respiratory syncytial virus (viral); it is probably the commonest cause.

cards.q: List the viral causes of bronchopneumonia.
cards.a: RSV (commonest), influenza, parainfluenza, adenoviruses, rhinovirus and measles.

cards.q: List the bacterial causes of bronchopneumonia.
cards.a: Staphylococcus pyogenes, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae and Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

cards.q: List the symptoms of bronchopneumonia.
cards.a: Fever, cough, breathlessness, anorexia, restlessness, cyanosis, upper abdominal pain, vomiting and diarrhoea; chest pain is unusual.

cards.q: List the signs of bronchopneumonia.
cards.a: Central trachea; resonant percussion notes equal on both sides; crepitations (fine or coarse); and diminished breath sounds.

cards.q: Why is chest pain unusual in bronchopneumonia?
cards.a: Because the patchy consolidation is scattered in the lung substance and often does not reach the parietal pleura.

cards.q: Why are the percussion notes resonant and equal in bronchopneumonia?
cards.a: Because the consolidation is patchy with normal aerated lung in between, on both sides, so air still dominates.

cards.q: Give the key differences between lobar and bronchopneumonia.
cards.a: Lobar: homogenous, usually bacterial, dull percussion, increased fremitus, bronchial breathing, pleuritic pain present. Bronchopneumonia: patchy, usually viral, resonant equal percussion, crepitations, chest pain unusual. Trachea is central in both.

cards.q: List the goals of investigation in pneumonia.
cards.a: To confirm the diagnosis; determine the severity; establish complications; exclude close differentials; determine the causative organism; and monitor response to treatment.

cards.q: List the radiological investigations in pneumonia.
cards.a: Chest X-ray, chest computed tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging.

cards.q: List the microbiological investigations in pneumonia.
cards.a: Nasal washings/nasopharyngeal swabs, blood culture, sputum culture, pleural fluid culture, bronchoscopic fluid/BAL washings, lung aspirate studies, serology, nucleic acid amplification test, and countercurrent immuno-electrophoresis of urine.

cards.q: What are the two main uses of the chest X-ray in pneumonia?
cards.a: To confirm the diagnosis, and to indicate a complication such as pleural effusion or empyema.

cards.q: What is the chest X-ray appearance of viral pneumonia?
cards.a: Hyperinflation with bilateral interstitial infiltrates and peribronchial cuffing.

cards.q: What is the chest X-ray appearance of pneumococcal pneumonia?
cards.a: Confluent lobar consolidation.

cards.q: List the radiological findings in pneumonia.
cards.a: Peribronchial infiltrates, lobar/segmental consolidation, air bronchograms, airspace obliteration, and hilar lymphadenopathy.

cards.q: What is an air bronchogram and what does it signify?
cards.a: A black branching air-filled bronchus seen against white exudate-filled alveoli; it signifies alveolar filling (consolidation) and excludes collapse and pleural effusion.

cards.q: What is the significance of blood culture in pneumonia?
cards.a: The definitive diagnosis of bacterial infection may require isolation of an organism from blood, but blood culture correctly identifies the organism in only 20-30% of patients.

cards.q: Why is sputum m/c/s unreliable in children?
cards.a: Because the sputum is contaminated by upper airway flora.

cards.q: Why are nasopharyngeal and throat swabs unreliable in children?
cards.a: They may identify only normal commensal organisms of the nasopharynx and throat.

cards.q: How long does growth of respiratory viruses in tissue culture take?
cards.a: 5-10 days.

cards.q: On what does definitive diagnosis of a bacterial pneumonia depend?
cards.a: Isolation of an organism from the blood, pleural fluid or lung.

cards.q: What is the value of countercurrent immuno-electrophoresis of urine?
cards.a: It identifies bacterial antigens of capsulated bacteria like S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae, even after antimicrobial treatment has begun.

cards.q: What does the nucleic acid amplification test (e.g. PCR) do?
cards.a: Provides rapid detection of pathogens difficult to culture, using respiratory secretions, lung aspirate samples or blood.

cards.q: How is Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection diagnosed?
cards.a: On the basis of a positive PCR test or seroconversion in an IgG assay.

cards.q: What serological marker is useful in group A streptococcal pneumonia?
cards.a: The anti-streptolysin O (ASO) titre.

cards.q: What is the WBC picture in viral pneumonia?
cards.a: The count can be normal or elevated but usually not higher than 20,000/mm³, with a lymphocyte predominance.

cards.q: What is the WBC picture in bacterial pneumonia?
cards.a: Often elevated, in the range of 15,000-40,000/mm³, with a predominance of granulocytes.

cards.q: What does arterial blood gas analysis assess in pneumonia?
cards.a: The degree of hypoxia and the need for oxygen administration.

cards.q: Why is serial pulse oximetry valuable?
cards.a: It is non-invasive, instant and repeatable, and the trend across readings shows whether the child is improving or deteriorating.

cards.q: List the differential diagnoses of pneumonia.
cards.a: Bronchiolitis, bronchial asthma, bronchitis, bronchiectasis, lung abscess, pulmonary oedema, atelectasis due to foreign body or thick mucus, and chemical pneumonitis.

cards.q: How do you distinguish bronchiolitis from bronchopneumonia?
cards.a: Bronchiolitis is typically under 12 months with a dominant wheeze and marked hyperinflation but no consolidation; bronchopneumonia has dominant crepitations with patchy consolidation on X-ray.

cards.q: How do you distinguish asthma from pneumonia?
cards.a: Asthma is recurrent and episodic with complete recovery between attacks, triggers, atopy, reversibility with bronchodilators, wheeze rather than crepitations, and is usually afebrile.

cards.q: What clue points to an inhaled foreign body rather than pneumonia?
cards.a: Sudden onset of choking while eating in a previously well child, unilateral signs, mediastinal shift, and obstructive hyperinflation on an expiratory film.

cards.q: List the acute complications of pneumonia (SHARE 4Ps).
cards.a: Septicaemia, Heart failure, Atelectasis, Respiratory failure, Empyema, Pleural effusion, Pyo-pneumothorax, Pneumothorax, Pneumatocele (and subcutaneous emphysema, rare except in measles).

cards.q: List the chronic complications of pneumonia.
cards.a: Lung abscess and bronchiectasis.

cards.q: What is a pneumatocele and which organism is classically associated with it?
cards.a: A thin-walled air-filled cyst in the lung formed by a ball-valve effect in necrotic lung; classically associated with Staphylococcus aureus.

cards.q: In which infection is subcutaneous emphysema seen as a complication of pneumonia?
cards.a: Measles infection (giant cell pneumonia).

cards.q: Explain the progression from pleural effusion to pyo-pneumothorax.
cards.a: Pleural inflammation exudes fluid (effusion); the fluid becomes infected and purulent (empyema); the lung ruptures into the infected space giving pus and air together (pyo-pneumothorax).

cards.q: Why does bronchiectasis develop after pneumonia?
cards.a: It is the result of partial (incomplete) resolution, with residual inflammation and destruction of the bronchial wall.

cards.q: State the three principles of treatment of pneumonia.
cards.a: Eradicate the infection; give supportive treatment; and manage the complications.

cards.q: What constitutes specific treatment of pneumonia?
cards.a: Antibiotics, antivirals and antifungal drugs.

cards.q: By what is antimicrobial treatment of pneumonia guided?
cards.a: Age of the patient, immune status, local epidemiologic information, radiologic findings, and microbiology results if available.

cards.q: List the supportive treatments for pneumonia.
cards.a: Hospital admission when necessary; oxygen therapy with ABG monitoring; antipyretics; fluid therapy; trial of bronchodilators; nursing care; appropriate feeding; and chest physiotherapy (percussion, postural drainage, tracheal suction).

cards.q: Which single supportive measure is most important in pneumonia?
cards.a: Oxygen therapy, because it directly addresses the failure of oxygen exchange across the alveolar membrane.

cards.q: List the indications for hospitalization in pneumonia.
cards.a: Age <6 months; sickle cell anaemia with acute chest syndrome; multiple lobe involvement; immunocompromised state; toxic appearance; severe respiratory distress; requirement for supplemental oxygen; dehydration; vomiting; no response to appropriate oral antibiotics; and noncompliant parents.

cards.q: What are the three specific treatments for complications your lecturer lists?
cards.a: Digitalize in CCF; drain pleural fluid; and transfuse if the patient is anaemic.

cards.q: Why are antipyretics important in pneumonia, beyond comfort?
cards.a: Fever raises the metabolic oxygen demand, which a hypoxic child cannot afford.

cards.q: Outline the prevention of pneumonia.
cards.a: Immunization against respiratory pathogens (measles, diphtheria, pertussis, H. influenzae); health education (reduce overcrowding, adequate nutrition, breastfeeding, avoid kissing children when having an RTI, reduce cigarette and wood smoke); and legislation to reduce environmental pollution and discourage smoking.

cards.q: Which immunizations help prevent pneumonia?
cards.a: Vaccines against measles, diphtheria, pertussis and H. influenzae (and pneumococcal vaccine against Strep pneumoniae).

cards.q: Why is legislation needed in pneumonia prevention?
cards.a: Because structural risks like environmental pollution and tobacco smoke cannot be avoided by an individual family and require government action.

cards.q: OSPE — What is the golden rule when presenting a chest X-ray?
cards.a: Assess details and technical adequacy first, then read systematically (ABCDE), and give the diagnosis last — never start by describing the obvious abnormality.

cards.q: OSPE — What does RIPE stand for in assessing X-ray adequacy?
cards.a: Rotation (clavicles equidistant from spinous processes), Inspiration (5-6 anterior ribs in a child), Penetration (vertebrae just visible behind the heart), and Everything included.

cards.q: OSPE — What does the ABCDE system for reading a chest X-ray stand for?
cards.a: Airway (trachea), Breathing (lung fields zone by zone), Circulation (heart and mediastinum), Diaphragm (and costophrenic angles), Everything else (bones, soft tissues, lines).

cards.q: OSPE — Why can't you reliably comment on heart size on a paediatric AP film?
cards.a: Because the AP projection magnifies the heart, so apparent cardiomegaly may be artefactual.

cards.q: OSPE — A rotated or expiratory film can mimic what abnormality?
cards.a: Rotation makes one lung look whiter (false consolidation); an expiratory film crowds the bases and enlarges the heart (false consolidation and false cardiomegaly).

cards.q: OSPE — A homogenous opacity with a sharp upper border and air bronchograms, trachea central. Diagnosis?
cards.a: Lobar (e.g. right lower lobe) pneumonia; the sharp border is a fissure and the air bronchogram confirms alveolar consolidation.

cards.q: OSPE — What does an air bronchogram allow you to exclude?
cards.a: Collapse (blocked airway, no air in bronchus) and pleural effusion (fluid outside the lung) — because in both there would be no air-filled bronchus within the opacity.

cards.q: OSPE — Bilateral patchy opacities, hyperinflation with flat diaphragms and 8 anterior ribs, peribronchial cuffing. Diagnosis and likely organism in an infant?
cards.a: Viral bronchopneumonia; in an infant following coryza the likely organism is RSV.

cards.q: OSPE — How is hyperinflation identified on a chest X-ray?
cards.a: Flattened diaphragms and more than 6 anterior ribs visible above the diaphragm.

cards.q: OSPE — Why does viral infection cause hyperinflation?
cards.a: Inflammation narrows the small airways; air enters on inspiration but cannot escape on expiration, causing air trapping.

cards.q: OSPE — What is peribronchial cuffing?
cards.a: Thickened, oedematous bronchial walls seen end-on as a thick dark ring (doughnut), indicating airway-centred (viral) pathology.

cards.q: OSPE — Which film should you request if you suspect an inhaled foreign body?
cards.a: An expiratory film — the obstructed side stays inflated (ball-valve) while the normal lung empties, making the difference obvious.

cards.q: CLINICAL — What respiratory rate defines tachypnoea in a 1-5 year old (WHO)?
cards.a: More than 40 breaths per minute.

cards.q: CLINICAL — What signs indicate severe pneumonia?
cards.a: Tachypnoea, hypoxaemia (low SpO2), and increased work of breathing — subcostal recession, nasal flaring, grunting; plus a toxic appearance.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why is a falling respiratory rate with a calm, drowsy child alarming in pneumonia?
cards.a: It signals exhaustion and impending respiratory failure — a pre-terminal sign — not improvement. Look for rising CO2 on ABG.

cards.q: CLINICAL — In a severe pneumonia with SpO2 88%, what is the first action?
cards.a: Give oxygen immediately, before completing the history, then continue assessment with serial pulse oximetry and ABG.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A child with pneumonia deteriorates with stony dullness, absent breath sounds and trachea deviated to the opposite side. What has happened?
cards.a: A large pleural effusion or empyema has developed — a space-occupying collection pushing the trachea away.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why does an empyema not respond to antibiotics alone?
cards.a: Antibiotics penetrate a pleural collection (pus in a closed space) poorly; the treatment is drainage, not a stronger antibiotic.

cards.q: CLINICAL — How do you manage a suspected empyema?
cards.a: Chest X-ray and ultrasound to confirm and assess loculation; diagnostic and therapeutic thoracocentesis with fluid sent for microscopy, culture, Gram stain, protein, glucose and cytology; drainage; escalate antibiotics per culture; and consider TB.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why is pleural fluid a valuable microbiological specimen?
cards.a: Because definitive bacterial diagnosis can be made from pleural fluid, which has a much higher yield than blood culture's 20-30%.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A previously well toddler chokes on groundnuts, then presents 2 weeks later with fever and reduced air entry on one side. Diagnosis?
cards.a: Inhaled foreign body with secondary post-obstructive pneumonia.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why must a foreign body be removed by bronchoscopy rather than treated with antibiotics alone?
cards.a: The obstruction causes stasis and recurrent infection; antibiotics cannot resolve the pneumonia while the foreign body and obstruction remain.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why should you always ask about a choking episode in a child with unilateral chest signs?
cards.a: Because after aspiration there is a silent interval when the child seems well and the choking is often not volunteered, so the foreign body is easily missed.

cards.q: CLINICAL — What does a mediastinal shift tell you in a child you thought had pneumonia?
cards.a: It indicates a complication or alternative diagnosis — collapse (trachea towards), or effusion/pneumothorax/obstructive hyperinflation (trachea away) — because simple consolidation keeps the trachea central.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Which important condition mimics pneumonia in a sickle cell child and how is it managed?
cards.a: Acute chest syndrome — a medical emergency managed with oxygen, hydration, analgesia, antibiotics and often transfusion.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why must tuberculosis always be considered in a non-resolving pneumonia in our setting?
cards.a: Because Mycobacterium tuberculosis is a listed cause of both lobar and bronchopneumonia and can present as pneumonia that fails to respond to standard antibiotics; send AFB/GeneXpert.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why does the appearance of crepitations over consolidation suggest improvement?
cards.a: Because as the exudate breaks up and air re-enters the previously solid alveoli, crepitations are heard — a sign of resolution rather than deterioration.
